workstreams.description: Find exports, functions, and variables that are never imported or called
Find duplicated logic that should be extracted into shared utilities
Find overly complex functions that should be simplified or split
Find exports that are never consumed outside their own module
Find architectural violations: wrong-direction imports, tight coupling, missing abstractions
Find security, correctness, and regression risk issues to harden

Verify feature correctness: logic bugs, incorrect calculations, wrong business logic, missing edge case handling, silent failures, incorrect state transitions, off-by-one errors, incorrect conditionals
Find performance issues: N+1 patterns, unnecessary re-renders, inefficient data structures, blocking operations, missing memoization, over-fetching, expensive computations in render paths

Find code quality and refactoring opportunities: SOLID violations, god objects, files that are too large to reason about, SRP violations, missing abstractions that would simplify multiple callsites, DRY/YAGNI/LoD principle breaches

findings.description: `checkApiHealth` is an exported runtime helper with no references outside or inside its own declaration site. The repo-wide `rg` search only finds the export line, so this is dead executable code rather than just an unnecessary export.
findings.recommended_action: Delete `checkApiHealth` from `frontend/src/api.ts`; if a health probe is still intended, add a real caller before keeping the helper.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/App.tsx`, `frontend/src/ui/ConnectionStatusBanner.tsx`, `frontend/src/ui/GitHubRepoPicker.tsx`, and `frontend/src/ui/MainMenu.tsx` all import `* as api`, yet `rg` still finds no `checkApiHealth` member access anywhere.

Health-adjacent helpers in the same module are live, for example `primePublicWriteSession()` is called from `frontend/src/ui/ConnectionStatusBanner.tsx`, so this is an isolated orphan export rather than an unused module.

findings.description: `fetchDistrictRoom` is an exported runtime helper that is never called anywhere in the repo. The only repo-wide hit is its own declaration, which makes it dead executable code.
findings.evidence: {'code_snippet': 'export async function fetchDistrictRoom(districtId: string): Promise<RoomState | null> {\n    return apiFetch<RoomState>(`/api/districts/${encodeURIComponent(districtId)}/room`);\n}', 'rg_command': "rg --glob '!node_modules' -n -w 'fetchDistrictRoom' .", 'rg_output': './frontend/src/api.ts:542:export async function fetchDistrictRoom(districtId: string): Promise<RoomState | null> {'}
findings.recommended_action: Delete `fetchDistrictRoom` from `frontend/src/api.ts`; if an initial room read is still needed, wire it into the presence flow instead of keeping an unused wrapper.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/App.tsx` imports `* as api` and actively calls `api.districtHeartbeat(...)` for room presence updates, but never `api.fetchDistrictRoom(...)`.
The dead symbol sits in the same durable-object section as the live `districtHeartbeat` helper, so the unused part is the initial fetch wrapper rather than the district-room feature itself.

findings.description: `SnapshotFreshnessCopy` is exported but never referenced outside `snapshotFreshness.ts`. It only names the local return shape for `buildSnapshotFreshnessCopy` and `useSnapshotFreshnessCopy`, so the exported type is dead public surface.
findings.recommended_action: Remove `export` from `SnapshotFreshnessCopy`, or inline the object return type into the two local helpers if you do not want a named local interface.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/ui/MainMenu.tsx` and `frontend/src/ui/HUD.tsx` import `useSnapshotFreshnessCopy`, but neither imports `SnapshotFreshnessCopy`.
`frontend/src/ui/HUD.tsx` does import `type SnapshotSource` from the same module, which shows that this module's shared type surface is used selectively and this interface is not.

findings.description: `GitHubReadableRepoListResponse` is exported but never crosses a file boundary. It only appears in `api.ts` as the return type of `fetchGitHubReadableRepos`, so it is dead API surface rather than a live shared contract.
findings.recommended_action: Remove `export` from `GitHubReadableRepoListResponse` and keep it local to `api.ts`, or inline the response shape into `fetchGitHubReadableRepos`.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/ui/GitHubRepoPicker.tsx` calls `api.fetchGitHubReadableRepos(...)` but imports only `* as api`, so the wrapper type is not shared at the call site.

`GitHubReadableRepo` from the same module is imported in `frontend/src/store/gameStore.ts`, `frontend/src/localSmokeBridge.ts`, and `frontend/src/ui/selectedRepoStatusCopy.ts`, which makes this unused wrapper a specific leftover rather than an unused module.

findings.description: `RepoRefreshStatusCopy` is exported but only used within `repoRefreshCopy.ts` to annotate the return type of `buildRepoRefreshStatusCopy`. No external file imports the interface, so the export is dead surface area.
findings.recommended_action: Remove `export` from `RepoRefreshStatusCopy`, or inline the return type of `buildRepoRefreshStatusCopy` if you do not need a named local interface.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/ui/MainMenu.tsx` imports `buildRepoRefreshStatusCopy` and consumes the returned object directly; it never imports `RepoRefreshStatusCopy`.
`frontend/src/ui/HUD.tsx` imports `buildRepoHudRefreshNotice` from the same module, but no file imports `RepoRefreshStatusCopy`, so only the producer module knows the type name.

findings.description: `SelectedRepoStatusCopy` is exported but never referenced outside `selectedRepoStatusCopy.ts`. It only names the local return shape of `buildSelectedRepoStatusCopy`, so the export is dead API surface.
findings.recommended_action: Remove `export` from `SelectedRepoStatusCopy`, or inline the return type in `buildSelectedRepoStatusCopy` if you want to keep the shape purely local.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/ui/MainMenu.tsx` and `frontend/src/ui/GitHubRepoPicker.tsx` both import `buildSelectedRepoStatusCopy`, but neither imports `SelectedRepoStatusCopy`.
The same file already keeps `SelectedGitHubRepoIngestStateLike` private, which matches the same 'helper-only shape stays local' pattern.

findings.description: `GitHubTokenExchangeResponse` is exported but only used inside `api.ts` as the return and generic type for `exchangeGitHubOAuthCode`. No external file references the interface, so the exported type name is dead contract surface.
findings.recommended_action: Remove `export` from `GitHubTokenExchangeResponse` and keep it local to `api.ts`, or inline the response shape into `exchangeGitHubOAuthCode`.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/App.tsx` calls `api.exchangeGitHubOAuthCode(...)` during OAuth callback handling but never imports this interface.

Other `api.ts` types such as `GitHubReadableRepo` and `ApiRuntimeStatus` are actually imported elsewhere, which makes this unused response wrapper a specific leftover rather than evidence that the whole type surface is dead.

findings.description: `RepoRefreshResponse` is exported but never referenced outside `api.ts`. It only annotates the local `refreshGitHubRepo` implementation, so the exported type name is dead public surface.
findings.recommended_action: Remove `export` from `RepoRefreshResponse` and keep it local to `api.ts`, or inline the response shape inside `refreshGitHubRepo`.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/ui/MainMenu.tsx` calls `api.refreshGitHubRepo(...)` without importing `RepoRefreshResponse`, so callers already rely on inference.
The shared refresh contract lives in `shared/repoRefresh.ts` and is imported into `api.ts`; this local wrapper is not the cross-file refresh type the rest of the app uses.

findings.description: `RepoRefreshNotice` is exported but only referenced inside `repoRefreshCopy.ts` as the return type of `buildRepoHudRefreshNotice`. No other file imports it, so the exported interface is dead surface area.
findings.recommended_action: Remove `export` from `RepoRefreshNotice`, or inline the return shape of `buildRepoHudRefreshNotice` if you want to avoid a named local interface.
edge_cases: `frontend/src/ui/HUD.tsx` imports `buildRepoHudRefreshNotice` and consumes the returned object shape directly; it never imports `RepoRefreshNotice`.
`ConnectedRepoRefreshStatus` from `shared/repoRefresh.ts` is imported into this file and across the UI, which makes this extra exported wrapper type the unused part.

findings.description: Heat-status classification is duplicated between the HUD and Repo City surface, and the copies have already diverged: the HUD switches to `watch` at 30 while the surface waits until 35.
findings.recommended_action: Extract one shared heat-status helper that returns the label and reuse it from both HUD and surface rendering.
edge_cases: A district with `heatLevel` 30-34 is shown as `watch` in the HUD (`districtStatusLabel`) but `stable` on the surface (`heatStatusLabel` and `heatStatusClassName`).
When `repoCityTransit` is active, both components are describing the same queued destination district, so the mismatch stays visible before the player even arrives.

findings.description: Transit path-length accumulation is implemented three times across HUD, Repo City surface, and the store, with each copy hand-rolling the same loop over path points.
findings.recommended_action: Move path-distance calculation into one shared utility and adapt callers to a single normalized point shape.
edge_cases: HUD builds `remainingTransitPath` from `pathPoints.slice(pathIndex)` plus `targetPosition`, while RepoCitySurface separately maps points into `{x,y}` objects; if one copy starts including or excluding the terminal point differently, the two overlays will disagree on remaining distance.

The store reuses the same math when evaluating route quality in `buildRoadGuidedTransitPath`, so a future bug fix in one copy can change routing behavior without updating the distance the player sees in HUD or surface UI.

findings.description: Current-waypoint distance calculation and `m` formatting are duplicated across the 3D waypoint label, the mission objective tracker, and the HUD direction arrow.
findings.recommended_action: Centralize waypoint-distance calculation and formatting so every mission UI surface reads from the same helper.
edge_cases: If the rounding threshold changes, the floating waypoint label, the objective tracker, and the HUD arrow can show different distances for the same active waypoint.

All three copies currently ignore the Y axis and handle missing waypoints differently (`return null`, `if (currentWp)`, `if (dist < 5) return null`), so adding vertical movement or alternate waypoint shapes would require synchronized fixes in multiple components.

findings.description: Mission triggers and active waypoints build near-identical repo-city markers: the same `calmColor` tint helper and the same ring/octahedron/beam composition are duplicated with only constant tweaks.
findings.evidence:
frontend/src/game/MissionTrigger.tsx:13-15,69-108
function calmColor(source: string, amount: number) {
    return `#${new THREE.Color(source).lerp(new THREE.Color('#c5d3e2'), amount).getHexString()}`;
}
...
<mesh ref={ringRef} rotation={[-Math.PI / 2, 0, 0]} position={[0, 0.05, 0]}>
    <ringGeometry args={[radius - 0.3, radius, 32]} />
    <meshStandardMaterial color={markerColor} emissive={glowColor} ... />
</mesh>
<mesh position={[0, 0.5, 0]}>
    <octahedronGeometry args={[0.4, 0]} />
    <meshStandardMaterial color={markerColor} emissive={glowColor} ... />
</mesh>
<mesh position={[0, 5, 0]}>
    <cylinderGeometry args={[0.03, 0.03, 10, 8]} />
    <meshStandardMaterial color={glowColor} emissive={glowColor} ... />
</mesh>

frontend/src/game/Waypoint.tsx:15-17,80-137
function calmColor(source: string, amount: number) {
    return `#${new THREE.Color(source).lerp(new THREE.Color('#c5d3e2'), amount).getHexString()}`;
}
...
<mesh ref={ringRef} rotation={[-Math.PI / 2, 0, 0]} position={[0, 0.06, 0]}>
    <ringGeometry args={[waypoint.radius - 0.5, waypoint.radius, 48]} />
    <meshStandardMaterial color={markerColor} emissive={beamColor} ... />
</mesh>
<group ref={markerRef} position={[0, 2.5, 0]}>
    <mesh>
        <octahedronGeometry args={[0.6, 0]} />
        <meshStandardMaterial color={markerColor} emissive={beamColor} ... />
    </mesh>
</group>
<mesh ref={beamRef} position={[0, 8, 0]}>
    <cylinderGeometry args={[0.06, 0.06, 16, 8]} />
    <meshStandardMaterial color={beamColor} emissive={beamColor} ... />
</mesh>
findings.recommended_action: Extract a shared repo-city marker primitive or a shared tint helper so visual tuning lands in one place.
edge_cases: Any repo-city art-direction change to opacity, emissive intensity, or `DoubleSide` handling has to be repeated in both files or district mission triggers and active waypoints will drift visually in the same scene.
Both components derive visibility from radius-based geometry (`radius` vs `waypoint.radius`), so accessibility tuning for small markers will be easy to apply to one copy and forget in the other.

findings.description: The HUD and main menu each wrap `useSnapshotFreshnessCopy` in nearly identical presentational components that differ mostly by CSS class names and test ids.
findings.recommended_action: Create one shared snapshot-freshness component that accepts a small style variant instead of duplicating the wrapper markup.
edge_cases: The HUD version accepts `generatedAt: string | null | undefined` while the menu version requires `generatedAt: string`, so missing-timestamp handling can diverge if one wrapper changes and the other does not.
If `useSnapshotFreshnessCopy` adds another display field or state, both wrappers need parallel markup updates to keep menu and in-game provenance consistent.

findings.description: Selected GitHub repo status is modeled twice: `selectedRepoStatusCopy.ts` has a shared loading/error/eligible/listed-only state machine, but `MainMenu.tsx` rebuilds the same branches inline for CTA and auth-card copy.
findings.recommended_action: Promote selected-repo UI state into one shared derived model and have MainMenu, the picker, and any status cards render from that single source.
edge_cases: The loading and failure branches both vary on whether `connectedRepo` exists; adding a new fallback path in only one copy will desynchronize the main CTA copy from the picker/status-card copy.

Eligibility policy comes from `repoTranslationEligibility.ts`; if `internal` repos or another visibility state becomes eligible later, both the inline menu logic and the shared status builder must change together to avoid contradictory UI.

findings.description: Bootstrap snapshot parsing and smoke-harness snapshot construction both manually clone and normalize repo snapshots from the same sample JSON, but they do it in separate, already-diverging code paths.
findings.recommended_action: Extract one repo-snapshot normalization/cloning utility and have both bootstrap and smoke harness call it, with explicit options for truncation or commit override.
edge_cases: Bootstrap truncates to `MAX_BOOTSTRAP_MODULES` and filters `dependencyEdges` and `signals` to `allowedModuleIds`, while `buildSmokeConnectedRepo` keeps the full module graph; the same sample snapshot can therefore drive different districts, missions, and signals in normal and smoke flows.
If `RepoModel` gains another nested array or metadata field, both manual clone paths need updates or one environment will silently retain shared references or omit fields that the other preserves.

findings.description: `shared/repoCityGenerator.ts` is a god module that owns repo classification, signal grouping, mission/bot generation, district naming, layout, road generation, and the bridge back into gameplay types. That concentration makes almost every repo-city rule change cross-cut multiple responsibilities in one file.
findings.evidence: The file is 1389 LOC. Evidence of mixed responsibilities: `classifyRepoArchetype` at shared/repoCityGenerator.ts:775-829, `buildDistrictLabelPrefix` at :994-1033, `generateRoads` at :1150-1186, `generateCityFromRepo` at :1197-1265, and gameplay bridges `generatedCityToDistricts` / `generatedCityToMissions` / `generatedCityToConflicts` at :1286-1388 all live together. Example excerpts: `const hasWorkspaceConfig = hasTextHint(...)` and 5 archetype exits in `classifyRepoArchetype`; 8 sequential hint checks in `buildDistrictLabelPrefix`; `generateCityFromRepo` classifies, ranks modules, computes footprints, derives heat, builds roads, missions, bots, and timestamps output in one pass.
findings.recommended_action: Split the module into focused units such as `archetypeClassification`, `signalMissionGeneration`, `layoutGeneration`, `roadGeneration`, and `gameBridge`. Keep static lookup tables in separate config modules and have `generateCityFromRepo` only orchestrate pure helpers.
edge_cases: Adding a new `RepoSignalType` currently requires touching multiple unrelated sections in the same file: template selection, bot profiles, grouped copy, mission generation, and conflict bridging.
Changing archetype rules now spans classification, hub ordering, layout slots, district labels, and road readability in one module, so a layout tweak can easily regress mission or labeling behavior.

findings.description: `gameStore.ts` centralizes nearly all runtime concerns: gameplay state, waypoint persistence, repo-city routing, GitHub auth/selection, DO room sync, reward toasts, and Worker API hydration. The store has become both a domain model and a controller layer, which makes invariants hard to reason about and hard to reuse safely.
findings.evidence: The file is 940 LOC. `GameState` at frontend/src/store/gameStore.ts:90-196 includes phase/player/district/capture/missions/factions/events/conflicts/UI/repo-city/GitHub auth/API concerns in one interface. Pure routing logic such as `buildRoadGuidedTransitPath` lives inside the store file at :254-399. The store body at :428-939 then also owns movement, capture, mission transitions, boss resolution, repo load/clear, auth resets, district room merging, and `loadFromApi`.
findings.recommended_action: Break the store into slices or domain modules: movement/transit, mission progression, repo-city session, GitHub auth/repo selection, and API sync. Move pure helpers like `buildRoadGuidedTransitPath` into standalone modules and expose only narrow store actions.
edge_cases: A new repo-load entry point must keep `loadRepoCity`, `clearRepoCity`, auth reset paths, API hydration, and smoke-mode setup in sync, because all of them mutate overlapping state in this one file.

Mission flow changes must also preserve waypoint persistence and timer resets (`saveWaypointState`, `clearWaypointState`, `missionTimer` updates), so a small mission tweak can silently break reload or boss transitions.

findings.description: The selected-GitHub-repo/auth state machine is duplicated across the store, UI controllers, copy helpers, and smoke harness instead of being owned by one canonical reducer/action set. This creates multiple sources of truth for the same transitions and increases race-condition surface area.
findings.evidence: Repeated resets and direct mutations appear in multiple places. In frontend/src/store/gameStore.ts:751-803, `selectedGitHubRepo`, `selectedGitHubRepoEligibility`, `selectedGitHubRepoIngestState`, and `selectedGitHubRepoSnapshot` are hand-reset in the initial state, `setGitHubAccessToken`, `setGitHubAuthError`, and `clearGitHubAuth`. In frontend/src/localSmokeBridge.ts:165-205 and :263-310, the smoke harness bypasses store actions with `useGameStore.setState({...})` and rewrites the same fields directly. In frontend/src/ui/GitHubRepoPicker.tsx:215-299, `handleRepoSelection` aborts requests, clears snapshot, resets ingest state, starts fetch, and writes error/success. In frontend/src/ui/selectedRepoStatusCopy.ts:74-215, status is recomputed again from the same inputs through branching helper functions.
findings.recommended_action: Introduce a single reducer-like module or explicit action helpers for repo selection/auth transitions, e.g. `resetSelectedRepoState`, `beginRepoIngest`, `repoIngestFailed`, `activateRepoSnapshot`. Make the smoke bridge call those actions instead of `useGameStore.setState`, and let UI read derived status from one canonical selector.
edge_cases: If a new field is added to selected-repo state, it must be updated in every hand-built reset object in `gameStore.ts` and `localSmokeBridge.ts`, or stale state will survive some transitions but not others.

`GitHubRepoPicker` guards in-flight ingest with `useGameStore.getState().selectedGitHubRepo`, but other code paths can change the same selection via direct store writes, so race handling is distributed instead of centralized.

findings.description: Classic mode and repo-city mode are implemented as pervasive inline branches inside the same components instead of separate compositions. This spreads control flow and styling decisions across the app and makes every feature change a two-mode audit.
findings.evidence: Search results show the same split across many files: App.tsx:311/372/376; CityScene.tsx:15/43/331/357; Player.tsx:39-48/128/187; Waypoint.tsx:68-172; HUD.tsx:152/214/218/312/388/447/520; MainMenu.tsx:297-651; GitHubRepoPicker.tsx:330-491; ConnectionStatusBanner.tsx:44-50; RewardToast.tsx:9-12; Leaderboard.tsx:19-55. Representative code: `repoCityMode ? (...) : (...)` blocks dominate HUD and MainMenu, while Player, Waypoint, and CityScene branch behavior and visuals on the same flag.
findings.recommended_action: Move mode-specific behavior behind separate components or mode adapters, e.g. `RepoCityHUD` vs `ClassicHUD`, `RepoCityMenu` vs `ClassicMenu`, and shared presentational primitives. Keep mode-specific copy/style maps centralized rather than branching inline in many files.
edge_cases: A new repo-city-only change now requires auditing every `repoCityMode` branch site to keep classic mode stable, because the two modes share modules instead of boundaries.
Behavior can drift between surfaces because each file chooses its own repo-city/classic split; for example HUD, MainMenu, Player, and CityScene all branch independently on the same flag.

findings.description: High-frequency player movement writes are pushed through the global store, while many components subscribe to the entire store with `useGameStore()` and no selector. That combination creates a broad rerender fan-out and makes performance harder to predict as more UI gets added.
findings.evidence: `commitPosition` at frontend/src/game/Player.tsx:84-111 calls `setPlayerPosition(nextPosition)` and `setCurrentDistrict(nextDistrict)`. `useFrame` at :114-211 invokes `commitPosition` during movement. Full-store subscriptions are widespread: Camera.tsx:22, Waypoint.tsx:24, CaptureOverlay.tsx:4, MissionTrigger.tsx:22/122, App.tsx:59/253, MissionPanel.tsx:18, MergeConflictGame.tsx:37, CityBulletin.tsx:4, ConnectionStatusBanner.tsx:5-14, Leaderboard.tsx:4, MissionObjectiveTracker.tsx:5-13, RewardToast.tsx:4 all call `useGameStore()` without a selector.
findings.recommended_action: Keep per-frame movement state local to the scene layer or a narrow transient slice, and expose throttled/derived state to the global store. Convert broad `useGameStore()` consumers to selectors so unrelated overlays do not rerender on every position update.
edge_cases: Holding movement keys causes `setPlayerPosition` to fire every frame, so panels like `ConnectionStatusBanner`, `CityBulletin`, and `RewardToast` can rerender even though they do not need player coordinates.
Crossing district boundaries triggers `setCurrentDistrict` from the same movement loop, so the app pays both per-frame position updates and additional broad store updates during transit.

findings.description: `App.tsx` is acting as a root controller instead of a thin composition shell. It mixes DO polling, repo refresh polling, OAuth callback exchange, smoke harness installation, keyboard shortcuts, mission timer behavior, and top-level rendering in one file.
findings.evidence: `DistrictRoomPoller` at frontend/src/App.tsx:58-95 mixes local capture ticking with DO heartbeat requests. `ConnectedRepoRefreshPoller` at :97-227 handles visibility/offline backoff, abort controllers, repo identity guards, polling cadence, and store writes. The `App` component at :255-356 bootstraps API hydration, runtime status subscription, GitHub OAuth callback exchange, smoke bridge installation, write-session priming, keyboard shortcuts, and mission timer logic before rendering the scene and overlays at :358-400.
findings.recommended_action: Extract root concerns into dedicated hooks/components such as `useDistrictRoomPolling`, `useConnectedRepoRefreshPolling`, `useGitHubOAuthCallback`, and `useMissionTimer`. Keep `App` focused on scene/UI composition.
edge_cases: Changing mount order or cleanup behavior now risks unrelated systems because polling, OAuth, and timer effects all live beside each other in the same root module.
Debugging stale intervals or abort-controller leaks spans multiple nested hooks in one file, especially when refresh polling and district-room polling are both active.

findings.description: `MainMenu` is an oversized controller component that owns refresh orchestration, seeded repo selection, GitHub auth/status modeling, repo selector toggles, and a very large mode-dependent render tree. Its responsibilities exceed simple view composition and make the menu hard to change safely.
findings.evidence: The file is 662 LOC. At frontend/src/ui/MainMenu.tsx:108-125, `MainMenu` pulls many store slices plus local refresh state. `handleRefreshRepo` at :154-225 owns controller lifecycle, network call, optimistic status, snapshot activation, and error reporting. The render body at :296-659 includes repo hero, snapshot freshness, refresh feedback, selected-repo status, start-button copy, GitHub auth card, GitHub picker launch, seeded repo selector, classic mode switch, and embedded `GitHubRepoPicker`.
findings.recommended_action: Split the component into hooks and subviews such as `useRepoRefresh`, `RepoHero`, `SelectedRepoStatusPanel`, `MenuActions`, and `SeedRepoSelector`. Keep derived status assembly out of the JSX-heavy component.
edge_cases: Refreshing repo behavior is coupled to menu rendering and selected-repo copy, so a refresh-flow change can break status presentation or start-button wording in the same component.
`selectedGitHubRepoIsActive` and status-copy assembly are recomputed here separately from the picker/store paths, so menu and picker behavior can drift if one side changes first.

findings.description: Transit and district-presentation logic is duplicated between `HUD.tsx` and `RepoCitySurface.tsx`. Both components independently resolve focused districts, rebuild path arrays, and compute distance labels, which increases the chance that the surface and HUD disagree about the same route.
findings.evidence: In frontend/src/ui/HUD.tsx:149-192, HUD resolves `transitGeneratedDistrict`, `transitDistrict`, `remainingTransitPath`, and `distanceLabel: formatTransitDistance(measure2DPathDistance([...]))`. In frontend/src/ui/RepoCitySurface.tsx:109-198, the surface separately resolves `currentSurfaceDistrict`, `currentMissionDistrict`, `transitDistrict`, `hoveredDistrict`, and computes guidance distances with `measure2DPathDistance([...])` in three branches. Both files also do repeated `districts.find(...)` / `generatedCity.districts.find(...)`, and each defines its own distance formatter (`formatTransitDistance` at HUD:28-31 vs `formatDistance` at RepoCitySurface:49-52).
findings.recommended_action: Introduce a shared presenter utility for repo-city transit state, e.g. `deriveTransitPresentation` and `resolveFocusedDistrict`, and reuse one distance-formatting helper across HUD and surface.
edge_cases: If transit distance semantics change, both `HUD` and `RepoCitySurface` must be updated or the HUD can report a different remaining distance than the map line shows.
If district-focus precedence changes in only one file, the HUD can highlight one district while the surface pins another because each computes queued/current/hovered/mission precedence independently.

findings.description: `api.ts` mixes multiple transport contracts in one module: some calls return `null`, some throw, some update global runtime status, and some bypass that status entirely. That inconsistency increases cognitive load for every caller and makes error handling easy to get wrong.
findings.evidence: `request` and `apiFetch` at frontend/src/api.ts:117-140 update `emitApiRuntimeStatus` and return `null` on transport failure. `writeApiFetch` at :187-217 also returns `null` on non-ok responses except for the 401 retry path. By contrast, `fetchGitHubReadableRepos` at :387-430 bypasses `request`, uses raw `fetch`, and throws `Error`, while `refreshGitHubRepo` / `fetchGitHubRepoRefreshStatus` at :456-525 also throw structured errors. `ConnectionStatusBanner` reads store-driven runtime status from frontend/src/ui/ConnectionStatusBanner.tsx:5-14 and :80-127, so raw GitHub fetch failures do not flow through the same status mechanism.
findings.recommended_action: Standardize the client contract: either return typed `Result` objects everywhere or throw typed errors everywhere. Route GitHub transport through the same status-aware request layer so callers do not need endpoint-specific error semantics.
edge_cases: `GitHubRepoPicker` must catch thrown errors from `fetchGitHubReadableRepos`, while store code calling `fetchDistricts` or `acceptMission` expects `null`; a new caller can easily implement the wrong failure path.
A GitHub repo-list failure will not update the same runtime-status channel used by `ConnectionStatusBanner`, so transport health and UI health indicators can diverge.

findings.description: `generateCityFromRepo` claims to be part of a deterministic translation layer, but it injects wall-clock time directly into the result. That hidden impurity makes caching, memoization, and behavioral reasoning harder because identical repo input does not produce identical city output.
findings.evidence: The file header at shared/repoCityGenerator.ts:1-3 says `Pure, deterministic translation layer: RepoModel -> GeneratedCity.` But `generateCityFromRepo` returns `generatedAt: new Date().toISOString()` at shared/repoCityGenerator.ts:1255-1265, so repeated calls with the same `RepoModel` produce different `GeneratedCity` objects.
findings.recommended_action: Make `generatedAt` an explicit input from the caller, or reuse `repo.generatedAt` when appropriate. Keep the generator referentially transparent for the same repo snapshot.
edge_cases: Calling `generateCityFromRepo` twice for the same snapshot yields different `generatedAt` values, which defeats simple equality checks and can trigger avoidable downstream re-renders.
Any test or cache keyed on `GeneratedCity` shape becomes unstable because the generator's output changes even when repo modules, edges, and signals do not.

current_slice_recommendation.title: Scan frontend/src and shared for unused module exports

known_risks: Codex may hallucinate evidence — always verify file_path exists before storing finding
Fix mode is skipped in dry_run mode (no commits) but Codex still runs
hardening workstream (q-006) is deferred until higher-priority workstreams complete at least one cycle
decisions.json in docs/review_artifacts/ can be edited manually to mark findings as keep or defer
